eli3: Simple linear regression models the relationship between one predictor X and a response Y as Y = β₀ + β₁X + ε, where ε ~ N(0,σ²). The OLS estimators β̂₀ and β̂₁ minimize Σ(yᵢ − β̂₀ − β̂₁xᵢ)² — the sum of squared residuals. They are the Gauss-Markov BLUE (Best Linear Unbiased Estimators) under the classical assumptions.

The key assumptions (Gauss-Markov): (1) linearity: E[Y|X] = β₀ + β₁X; (2) homoscedasticity: Var(ε) = σ² constant; (3) no autocorrelation: Cov(εᵢ,εⱼ) = 0; (4) exogeneity: E[ε|X] = 0. Normality of ε is needed for exact finite-sample inference (t and F tests) but not for OLS consistency. Adding outlier plots, residuals vs fitted, Q-Q plots diagnoses these assumptions.

R² = 1 − SS_res/SS_tot measures the proportion of variance in Y explained by X. R² ∈ [0,1]; R² = 1 means a perfect fit; R² = 0 means the model explains nothing more than the mean. Importantly, R² says nothing about whether the relationship is causal, whether the model is correctly specified, or whether predictions are reliable out-of-sample.

whenWhy.title: Quantifying the linear relationship between two variables
whenWhy.body: SLR provides a formal estimate of how Y changes per unit increase in X: β̂₁ with standard error, t-statistic, and confidence interval. This goes beyond correlation (which only measures direction and strength) by providing a directional effect estimate in units of Y. Use for: dose-response, price-demand, salary-experience relationships.

whenWhy.title: Prediction of new observations
whenWhy.body: The fitted model ŷ = β̂₀ + β̂₁x provides predictions for new x values. The prediction interval (PI) accounts for both uncertainty in the mean estimate and individual residual variability: PI = ŷ ± t_{n−2,α/2}·s·√(1 + 1/n + (x−x̄)²/Sxx). The PI is always wider than the CI for the mean. Extrapolation (predicting outside the range of training X) is risky.

whenWhy.title: Hypothesis testing about the slope
whenWhy.body: H₀: β₁ = 0 (X has no linear effect on Y). Under OLS assumptions: T = β̂₁/SE(β̂₁) ~ t(n−2). Rejecting H₀ provides evidence of a linear association. The F-test for overall fit (F = MS_reg/MS_res) is equivalent to the t-test for β₁ in SLR (F = T²) but generalizes to multiple regression.

deepDiveIntro: The OLS slope estimate is the sample covariance of x and y divided by the sample variance of x, and the intercept is chosen so the fitted line passes through the sample means. The slope is unbiased and its variance equals the error variance divided by the total sum of squares of x — more spread-out x values give more precise estimates. The Gauss-Markov theorem guarantees OLS is the minimum-variance linear unbiased estimator under linearity, homoscedasticity, and zero-mean errors, without requiring normality.

Adding a normality assumption for the errors gives the slope an exact normal distribution, which enables exact t-tests and confidence intervals using the residual standard error and n-minus-two degrees of freedom.
formula: β̂₁ = Sxy / Sxx = Σ(xᵢ−x̄)(yᵢ−ȳ) / Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²

deepDive.heading: OLS Estimators and Gauss-Markov
deepDive.body: Ordinary least squares fits the straight line that minimizes the total squared vertical error. Under the standard exogeneity and constant-variance assumptions, the resulting slope and intercept are the most efficient unbiased linear estimators. This is the main reason simple linear regression is such a central model in classical statistics.

deepDive.heading: Variance Decomposition and R²
deepDive.body: Regression divides total variation in the outcome into explained variation and leftover residual variation. The coefficient of determination summarizes what fraction of the original variation is captured by the fitted line. It is useful descriptively, but it should not be mistaken for evidence of causality or guaranteed predictive skill.

deepDive.heading: Inference on β₁ and β₀
deepDive.body: Once the model is fitted, you can test whether the slope is plausibly zero and build intervals for both the mean response and future observations. Confidence intervals for the average response are narrower because they only describe uncertainty in the line itself. Prediction intervals are wider because they must also include the irreducible scatter of individual outcomes around that line.

deepDive.heading: Residual Diagnostics
deepDive.body: Residual plots are the main way to check whether a fitted line is actually appropriate. Patterns in residuals can reveal curvature, changing variance, non-normal errors, and influential observations that distort the fit. Good regression practice therefore treats diagnostics as part of the model, not as an optional afterthought.

takeaways: β̂₁ = Sxy/Sxx, β̂₀ = ȳ − β̂₁x̄; Gauss-Markov: OLS is BLUE under linearity, homoscedasticity, exogeneity. [1]
R² = 1 − SS_res/SS_tot = r²_{xy}; R² alone does not validate the model. [1]
Inference: T = β̂₁/(s/√Sxx) ~ t(n−2); CI vs PI — PI always wider due to residual variance term. [1]
Residual diagnostics (residuals vs fitted, Q-Q, Cook's distance) check OLS assumptions. [1]
Extrapolation outside training X range is unreliable even with high in-sample R². [1]

edgeCases.q: What happens if X is random (not fixed by design)?
edgeCases.a: Gauss-Markov still holds conditional on X — conditioning on observed X values. If X is random, OLS estimators are still BLUE conditioned on X, and under joint normality of (X,Y), the t and F distributions are exact. The key assumption shifts from 'X is non-random' to E[ε|X] = 0 (strict exogeneity). If this fails (endogeneity: X is correlated with ε), OLS is biased and inconsistent — requires IV estimation. [1]

In OLS with an intercept, R² ∈ [0,1] because OLS always improves over the mean. However, if you impose β₀ = 0 (no intercept), or compute R² on a test set, R² can be negative — indicating the model predicts worse than the simple mean. Never compare R² between models with and without intercept, or between training and test sets without adjustment. [1]

sources.label: Montgomery, Peck & Vining — Introduction to Linear Regression Analysis (5th ed.), Ch. 2–3
Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 11
